annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState is refactored to be a generic, protocol-agnostic state container that only stores the parameters explicitly provided to it.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 31-36: new __init__(self, **kwargs) implementation that iterates over kwargs and sets them as instance attributes
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'refactor EcuState to be a generic, protocol-agnostic state container that only stores the parameters explicitly provided to it'. [3: Logical Necessity] Protocol-agnostic design requires no protocol-specific defaults baked into the class. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must refactor EcuState to only store explicitly provided parameters.

title: 1.1: Refactors EcuState to only store parameters explicitly provided to it using **kwargs in the constructor

annotations.description: Verifies that the __eq__ method is updated to compare all stored state attributes rather than only the hardcoded UDS-specific fields, ensuring protocol-agnostic equality checks.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 60-72: new __eq__ implementation that compares all keys in self.__dict__ with other.__dict__
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'The class should compare all stored state attributes for equality'. [2: Implicit Codebase Constraint] The original __eq__ only compared session, tp, misc, and security_level, ignoring dynamic state. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement equality comparison that covers all stored state.

title: 1.2: Implements equality comparison that compares all stored state (such as comparing all instance attributes)

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState provides a readable string representation with key-value pairs concatenated in alphabetical order by key for debugging purposes.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 55-58: new __repr__ implementation that iterates over sorted __dict__.items() to build the string
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'provide a readable string representation for debugging - for consistency, the repr should concatenate key-value pairs in alphabetical order by key (e.g., EcuState(a=1, b=2) produces "a1b2")'. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must provide a __repr__ that concatenates key-value pairs in alphabetical order.

title: 1.3: Implements __repr__ that concatenates sorted key-value pairs without separators (e.g., EcuState(a=1, b=2) returns 'a1b2')

annotations.description: Verifies that the Ecu class is modified to explicitly set UDS-style default parameters (session, security_level, communication_control) when initializing or resetting EcuState. Since EcuState is refactored to only store explicitly provided parameters, the Ecu class must now be responsible for providing UDS defaults.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 158-162: Ecu.reset() sets default UDS state (session=1, security_level=0, communication_control=0) after calling state.reset()
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'The existing Ecu class that uses EcuState should continue to work with UDS-style state parameters after the refactoring'. [2: Implicit Codebase Constraint] Since EcuState no longer provides default values, the Ecu class must be modified to inject UDS defaults explicitly. [3: Logical Necessity] Without Ecu class modifications, accessing session/security_level on a default Ecu() would raise AttributeError. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must modify the Ecu class to provide UDS-style default state.

title: 1.4: Modifies Ecu class to provide UDS-style default state (such as session=1, security_level=0) since EcuState no longer has defaults

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState supports dictionary-style read access to state attributes via bracket notation.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 44-46: implements __getitem__ returning self.__dict__[item]
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'support dictionary-style access to state attributes' and the interface specifies __getitem__. [3: Logical Necessity] Dictionary-style read access enables protocol-agnostic state retrieval. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement __getitem__ on EcuState.

title: 1.5: Implements dictionary-style read access to state attributes via __getitem__ in EcuState

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState supports dictionary-style write access to state attributes via bracket notation.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 51-53: implements __setitem__ setting self.__dict__[key] = value
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'support dictionary-style access to state attributes' and the interface specifies __setitem__. [3: Logical Necessity] Dictionary-style write access enables protocol-agnostic state modification. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement __setitem__ on EcuState.

title: 1.6: Implements dictionary-style write access to state attributes via __setitem__ in EcuState

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState supports checking whether another EcuState's values are contained within this state.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 74-85: implements __contains__ that checks if item's attribute values are found within self's iterable values
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'containment checks for partial state matching' and the interface specifies __contains__. [3: Logical Necessity] Containment checking enables protocol-agnostic partial state comparison. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement __contains__ on EcuState.

title: 1.7: Implements containment checking via __contains__ in EcuState

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState supports less-than comparison for ordering: states with fewer attributes are smaller, and for equal attribute counts, compares values in sorted key order.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 91-131: implements __lt__ based on attribute count and sorted key-value comparison
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'ordering for sorting (states with fewer attributes are smaller; for equal counts, compare values in sorted key order)'. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement __lt__ with this ordering logic.

title: 1.8: Implements __lt__ ordering by attribute count first, then by values in sorted key order

annotations.description: Verifies that __lt__ raises TypeError when comparing attributes of incompatible types.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 109-112: raises TypeError when attribute types don't match
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'raising TypeError for incompatible types' as part of the ordering requirement. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must raise TypeError when comparing incompatible types in __lt__.

title: 1.9: Raises TypeError in __lt__ when comparing attributes of incompatible types

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState has a command method that returns a recreatable string in the format EcuState(key=repr(value), ...) with keys sorted alphabetically.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 148-152: implements command() returning 'EcuState(' + sorted key=repr(value) pairs + ')'
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'serialize the state into a recreatable command string in the format EcuState(key=repr(value), ...) with keys sorted alphabetically' and the interface specifies command(). [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement command() returning the specified format with alphabetically sorted keys.

title: 1.10: Implements command() returning recreatable string in the format EcuState(key=repr(value), ...) with keys sorted alphabetically

annotations.description: Verifies that EcuState.reset() clears all stored state attributes, returning the instance to an empty state.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 142-146: reset() deletes all keys from __dict__
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'clearing all state via the existing reset method'. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must implement reset() that clears all stored state attributes.

title: 1.11: Refactors reset() to remove all attributes from the EcuState instance

annotations.description: Verifies that a docstring is added or updated for the EcuState class explaining its new protocol-agnostic design and how it stores state for different protocols.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py diff lines 26-30: adds docstring 'Stores the state of an Ecu. The state is defined by a protocol, for example UDS or GMLAN.'
annotations.justification: [3: Logical Necessity] A significant refactoring to support multiple protocols benefits from documentation explaining the new design. [4: Quality Necessity] Clear documentation helps future maintainers understand the class's purpose. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer should add or update documentation for the refactored EcuState class.

title: 2.1: Adds or updates docstring for EcuState explaining its protocol-agnostic design

annotations.description: Detects if the refactoring omits at least one existing public API method from EcuState. Answer YES if reset, __eq__, __ne__, __lt__, __hash__, or __repr__ is missing from the refactored EcuState class.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py: original EcuState has these public methods: reset, __eq__, __ne__, __lt__, __hash__, __repr__. Check if the agent's EcuState includes all of them.
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'clearing all state via the existing reset method' and 'existing Ecu class that uses EcuState should continue to work', implying API preservation. [3: Logical Necessity] Removing public API methods would break existing code that depends on them. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must NOT remove existing public API methods from EcuState.

title: 4.1: Omits at least one existing public API method from EcuState (reset, __eq__, __ne__, __lt__, __hash__, or __repr__)

annotations.description: Detects if EcuState.__init__ still contains hardcoded UDS-specific default values (such as session=1, security_level=0, communication_control=0) instead of being a generic container that only stores explicitly provided parameters.
annotations.evidence: gold.patch ecu.py: original code had def __init__(self, session=1, tester_present=False, security_level=0, communication_control=0, **kwargs). The refactored code should only have def __init__(self, **kwargs).
annotations.justification: [1: Explicit Prompt Requirement] The prompt states 'refactor EcuState to be a generic, protocol-agnostic state container that only stores the parameters explicitly provided to it'. [2: Implicit Codebase Constraint] The prompt explicitly identifies 'hardcoded fields like session, security_level, and communication_control that are specific to UDS' as the problem to fix. [5: Conclusion] Therefore, any correct answer must NOT keep hardcoded UDS-specific default values in EcuState.__init__.

title: 4.2: Keeps hardcoded UDS-specific default values in EcuState.__init__ (such as session=1, security_level=0)
